trending.title: EU Commission Opens Formal AI Act Inquiry into Anthropic's Unreleased Claude Mythos Model
trending.body: On 16 April 2026, the European Commission opened a formal AI Act inquiry into Anthropic's Claude Mythos, a pre-release cybersecurity frontier model capable of autonomously identifying and exploiting zero-day software vulnerabilities at superhuman levels. The inquiry tests the EU AI Office's pre-deployment authority under GPAI provisions, given that Anthropic has signed the voluntary GPAI Code of Practice, which mandates risk assessment and mitigation even for models not yet commercially offered in Europe. The Commission confirmed on 17 April that obligations apply extraterritorially to models that may affect EU services or rights. European regulators including Germany's BSI were sidelined from direct testing access — a gap that raises urgent governance questions about dual-use frontier models. The case is a landmark test of whether the EU can regulate sovereign AI capabilities and is directly relevant to Prosus portfolio companies with AI product surfaces (Ema, Clarity, Brainfish, CodeKarma, Advolve.AI) and foundation-model dependencies.

trending.title: CADE Opens Full Administrative Proceeding Against Google for AI Use of Journalistic Content
trending.body: On 23 April 2026, Brazil's CADE Tribunal unanimously (5–0) converted a long-running inquiry (since 2019) into a formal administrative proceeding (processo administrativo) against Google for alleged exploitative abuse of dominance through scraping and synthesising journalistic content in Google Search and AI Overviews without fair compensation or opt-out. Studies cited showed click-through rates to news sites dropping from 15% to 8% and zero-click sessions rising from 16% to 26% following AI-powered search summaries. Google holds over 90% of Brazil's search market. The case is cross-pillar (Competition + IP + AI Governance) and mirrors parallel probes in the EU, UK, and South Africa. The decision repositions publisher bargaining power and is directly relevant to iFood, Movile, and any Prosus entity reliant on Google-indexed digital traffic or generative AI-scraped content.
trending.source: Reuters / Folha de S.Paulo / CNN Brasil

trending.title: OLX Poland Fined PLN 24.3 Million by Warsaw Court for Algorithmic Rating Manipulation (DSA / Consumer Protection)
trending.body: On 16–18 April 2026, the Warsaw Court of Competition and Consumer Protection upheld — with a 15% reduction — the UOKiK fine against Prosus subsidiary OLX Group, setting the final penalty at PLN 24.3 million (approximately €5.7 million) for operating a misleading seller-rating algorithm that inflated positive scores between November 2020 and July 2024. The court confirmed UOKiK's finding that OLX's algorithm assigned disproportionate weight to positive ratings and misclassified mid-range scores as positive, without informing consumers. The ruling triggers compliance costs and third-party audit requirements for OLX's Central European operations and is one of the largest tech platform consumer-deception fines in Polish history. The decision is cross-pillar (Competition + DSA/Ecommerce) and has immediate relevance to Prosus's platform portfolio including eMAG and Takealot.
trending.source: UOKiK / Wirtualne Media / World Today News

trending.title: EU Digital Omnibus: GDPR Deidentification Reforms Under Negotiation — EDPB Warns of Rights Rollback
trending.body: The EU Digital Omnibus proposal (adopted 19 November 2025) remains in active trilogue negotiation as of April 2026, with contested provisions that would narrow the personal data definition in GDPR Article 4(1) to make pseudonymised data non-personal for entities that cannot identify the individual, and introduce a new Article 41a empowering the Commission to set implementing acts on pseudonymisation criteria. The EDPB and EDPS issued Joint Opinion 2/2026 in February 2026 strongly opposing the personal data redefinition, warning it goes beyond CJEU case law and improperly delegates scope decisions to the Commission. If adopted, the reform would facilitate AI model training with pseudonymised data — directly relevant to Prosus portfolio companies like Ema, Brainfish, and PayU that process large volumes of EU user data. Civil society groups including noyb and EDRi warn of systemic Big Tech benefit.

trending.title: DPC Ireland Fines TikTok €530 Million for Unlawful EEA Data Transfers to China
trending.body: On 2 May 2025, the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) issued its final decision fining TikTok Technology Limited €530 million — comprising €485 million for violating GDPR Article 46(1) (unlawful cross-border data transfers via SCCs without equivalent protection) and €45 million for transparency failures under Article 13(1)(f). The DPC ordered TikTok to suspend EEA data transfers to China within 6 months if not compliant. TikTok is appealing to the High Court of Ireland, which in November 2025 stayed the suspension order pending appeal. The case is one of the largest GDPR cross-border transfer enforcement actions ever and sets a precedent directly relevant to PayU, OLX, and any Prosus entity transferring EU user data to non-adequate countries.

competition.title: Prosus Sells 4.5% Delivery Hero Stake to Uber for €270M — EU Merger Remedy Compliance Step
competition.body: On 17 April 2026, Prosus announced the sale of 4.5% of Delivery Hero (13.6 million shares) to Uber at €20/share (€270 million, a 22% premium to VWAP), reducing its stake from 26.3% to 21.8%. This is a binding compliance step under the European Commission's conditional clearance of Prosus's acquisition of Just Eat Takeaway (August 2025), which required Prosus to divest its Delivery Hero stake below a 'very low percentage' within 12 months. Prosus confirmed the divestiture obligation remains ongoing. The transaction reshapes the competitive dynamics of European food delivery and creates a new structural link between Uber Eats and Delivery Hero.

competition.title: CADE Upholds Daily R$250,000 Fine Against Meta/WhatsApp for Non-Compliance on AI Chatbot Access
competition.body: Brazil's CADE Tribunal upheld (30 March 2026) the interim daily fine of R$250,000 (~€40,000/day) against Meta and WhatsApp for failing to comply with an interim measure requiring competitor access to WhatsApp's AI chatbot integration infrastructure. The case stems from concerns that WhatsApp's exclusive partnerships with AI chatbot providers foreclosed competition in Brazil's rapidly growing conversational commerce market. The fine accrues daily until compliance, making this one of the most financially consequential competition enforcement actions in Brazilian digital markets. Directly relevant to iFood's reliance on WhatsApp as a customer acquisition channel and Movile's messaging commerce investments.

competition.title: CADE Approves First Algorithmic Collusion Settlement (Intelprice Fuel Pricing Software)
competition.body: On approximately 8 April 2026, Brazil's CADE approved a cease-and-desist agreement (TCC) with Intelprice, a pricing software developer found to have facilitated price coordination among fuel retailers through its algorithmic pricing tool — the first such algorithmic collusion case resolved by Brazilian antitrust authorities. The settlement requires Intelprice to modify its software to prevent price-signalling between competitors and submit to compliance monitoring. The case establishes an important precedent for algorithmic pricing tools used in delivery, ecommerce, and platform contexts, with direct implications for iFood's restaurant pricing algorithms and any Prosus portfolio company using dynamic pricing software.

competition.title: Swiggy CCI Probe: Karnataka High Court Extends Stay on Antitrust Proceedings (April 21, 2026)
competition.body: On 21 April 2026, the Karnataka High Court extended its stay on the Competition Commission of India (CCI) proceedings against Swiggy, after the court found procedural concerns regarding the use of untested third-party evidence in the CCI's prima facie finding (November 2024) that Swiggy engaged in exclusivity agreements, platform self-preferencing, deep discounting, and excessive commission charges (25–35%). The next hearing is scheduled for 25 April 2026. Prosus holds approximately 17% of Swiggy. Separately, Swiggy faces Karnataka state-mandated welfare fee levies of 1% of order value (capped at Rs 0.5/transaction) for gig worker social security, effective February 2026.

competition.title: CMA Fines AA Driving School £4.2M — First DMCCA Direct Consumer Enforcement Penalty
competition.body: On 15 April 2026, the UK Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) fined Automobile Association Developments Ltd (AA/BSM Driving Schools) £4.2 million — reduced 40% for early settlement — and ordered £760,000 in refunds to over 80,000 learner drivers for drip pricing: a mandatory £3 booking fee was concealed until checkout in breach of the Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024. This is the first substantive financial penalty under the DMCCA's direct consumer enforcement powers (effective April 6, 2025). The ruling signals aggressive CMA consumer enforcement posture relevant to OLX, eMAG, Takealot, Meesho, and any Prosus ecommerce marketplace that uses dynamic checkout pricing.

competition.title: EC Finds Temu in Breach of DSA for Inadequate Illegal Product Risk Assessment
competition.body: In July 2025, the European Commission issued preliminary findings that Temu violated Article 34 of the Digital Services Act (DSA) by conducting an inadequate systemic risk assessment of illegal and unsafe products on its platform. Mystery-shopping tests found baby toys and small electronics that did not comply with EU safety standards. Temu — designated a VLOP with 92 million monthly EU users in 2024 — is exercising its rights of defense. A confirmed breach could result in fines up to 6% of global annual turnover. Parallel DSA probes into addictive design and recommender-system transparency remain open. Relevant to OLX, Takealot, Meesho, and eMAG as VLOP-adjacent marketplace operators subject to DSA obligations.

competition.title: ANPD Brazil Restructures as Full Regulatory Agency — Enforcement Capacity Boosted
competition.body: On 6 April 2026, Brazil enacted Decree 12.881/2026 and ANPD Resolution CD/ANPD No. 33, restructuring the National Data Protection Authority (ANPD) into a full independent regulatory agency with six new superintendencies and a dedicated audit unit. ANPD simultaneously published its Priority Themes Map 2026–2027 focusing on children's data, AI-driven targeted advertising, and algorithmic decision-making. This significantly increases LGPD enforcement capacity and signals imminent fines. Directly relevant to iFood, PayU, and any Prosus entity processing Brazilian consumer data at scale.

privacy.title: EDPB 2025 Annual Report: €1.15 Billion in GDPR Fines — AI, DMA & Cross-Border Transfer Focus
privacy.body: The EDPB's 2025 Annual Report (published 9 April 2026) confirmed that EU Data Protection Authorities imposed a combined €1.15 billion in GDPR fines during 2025, with Ireland's DPC accounting for €530.8 million (dominated by TikTok) and France's CNIL contributing €486.9 million across 84 decisions. The report highlighted a new cross-regulatory cooperation framework integrating GDPR enforcement with DMA, DSA, and AI Act proceedings, and flagged ongoing work on DPIA templates for AI systems. No binding decisions under Articles 65/66 were issued in 2025. Cross-border data transfer compliance — particularly to China — is identified as the top 2026 enforcement priority. PayU, OLX, and Swiggy each have exposure through large-scale EU data processing.

privacy.title: EDPB Announces Stakeholder Event on Competition and Data Protection Intersection
privacy.body: On 23 April 2026, the EDPB published a save-the-date notice for a stakeholder event on the intersection of competition law and data protection, reflecting growing regulatory coordination between DPAs and competition authorities across the EU. The event signals the EDPB's intent to develop guidance on how GDPR applies in competition investigations involving platform data access, algorithmic profiling, and data-driven market power. This is directly relevant to any Prosus portfolio company — particularly OLX, eMAG, and PayU — where competition regulators are increasingly requesting access to personal data held by dominant platforms.

privacy.title: US SECURE Data Act Introduced: Federal Privacy Bill Would Preempt 22+ State Privacy Laws
privacy.body: On 22 April 2026, US House Republicans introduced the SECURE Data Act (H.R. 8413), a comprehensive federal consumer privacy bill that would preempt state privacy laws across all 22+ states with existing frameworks. The bill grants consumers rights to access, delete, and opt-out of data sales and targeted advertising, requires parental consent for minors' data, mandates annual FTC registration for data brokers, and introduces data minimisation obligations. Enforcement falls exclusively to the FTC and state attorneys general — no private right of action. The bill exempts businesses under $25M annual revenue. First committee votes are expected May 2026. Relevant to DoorDash, Uber Eats, and Prosus-linked US ecommerce or payments operations.

privacy.title: ANPD, MPF, and Senacon Order Immediate Measures Against X for AI Tool Generating Improper Content
privacy.body: Brazil's data protection authority (ANPD), the Federal Public Ministry (MPF), and Senacon jointly ordered immediate corrective measures against X (formerly Twitter) for failures in an AI-powered content generation tool that produced improper content — including content that may harm minors. The order requires X to demonstrate technical safeguards, submit a compliance report within a specified deadline, and suspend the tool pending verification. The case signals Brazil's growing willingness to use multi-agency enforcement coalitions against AI-driven platform risks, consistent with ANPD's 2026–2027 Priority Themes Map. Relevant to Prosus portfolio companies deploying generative AI in consumer-facing applications.

privacy.title: South Korea Emerges as Model for AI Data Use via Pseudonymisation Governance
privacy.body: A 22 April 2026 IAPP analysis highlights South Korea's emerging privacy governance model, which uses pseudonymisation as a legal gateway to AI training and data use, offering organisations a structured path to process personal data without full anonymisation. The framework — distinct from GDPR's pseudonymisation classification as still-personal data — is gaining traction as a practical compliance model for AI developers. This is relevant to Prosus portfolio companies with AI product lines (Ema, Brainfish, CodeKarma) and to the EU Digital Omnibus debates on GDPR Article 4(1) redefinition.

privacy.title: EU Member States Push for Aligned Strategy on Social Media Age Restrictions
privacy.body: On 17 April 2026, IAPP reported that EU member states are seeking a coordinated approach to social media age restrictions amid global regulatory fragmentation, as multiple jurisdictions (Australia, France, UK, Florida) have enacted or proposed minimum age requirements for social media access. The EU is considering whether to harmonise through DSA secondary acts or through member-state action plans. This is relevant to Prosus platforms with youth user bases — including Brainly, GoodHabitz, GoStudent, and Eruditus — as well as to OLX and eMAG, which are subject to DSA obligations on minors' data.

ip.title: Thomson Reuters v. ROSS Intelligence: AI Training on Legal Database Fails Fair Use (Third Circuit Appeal Pending)
ip.body: On 11 February 2025, a Delaware court reversed its 2023 ruling and granted summary judgment to Thomson Reuters, finding that ROSS Intelligence's use of 25,000 Westlaw headnotes to train a competing non-generative AI legal search tool did not qualify as fair use. The court distinguished the case from generative AI rulings: ROSS's use was commercial, non-transformative (same legal research purpose as Westlaw), and directly harmed Westlaw's market and its potential AI training data licensing revenue. The case is on appeal to the Third Circuit. The ruling matters for Stack Overflow, Brainly, and Eruditus, whose content databases may be used to train competing AI tools.

ip.title: Bartz v. Anthropic: $1.5B Settlement After Court Rules Pirated Books Not Fair Use for AI Training
ip.body: In June 2025, a Northern District of California court ruled (Judge William Alsup) that Anthropic's use of lawfully purchased books to train Claude was 'exceedingly transformative' and constituted fair use, but that Anthropic's use of 7+ million pirated books from shadow libraries was not. A $1.5 billion settlement (approximately $3,000 per pirated book) followed in September 2025, with preliminary class approval in October 2025 and a fairness hearing scheduled for 23 April 2026. The case signals that generative AI companies face meaningful liability for pirated training data, while lawful acquisition provides a protected path. Relevant to Brainly, Stack Overflow, and Eruditus as content originators whose material may appear in shadow library datasets.

ip.title: Kadrey v. Meta: Fair Use for LLM Training on Shadow Library Books — Market Harm Key Battleground
ip.body: On 25–27 June 2025, a Northern District of California court granted summary judgment to Meta, ruling that training Llama LLMs on books from shadow libraries was 'highly transformative' fair use, with plaintiffs failing to demonstrate concrete market harm or evidence that LLM outputs substituted for the original works. The ruling is narrower than it appears: seeding and distribution claims proceed, and the court explicitly stated that 'market harm remains the most important fair use factor.' The judgment is significant for AI training data policy and is directly relevant to Prosus portfolio companies — including Brainly, Stack Overflow, and Eruditus — whose content may be scraped and used to train competing models.
ip.source: Baker Botts / JDSupra / Fahey IP Law

ip.title: WIPO Director General Daren Tang Appointed for Second Term
ip.body: On 21 April 2026, WIPO announced that Director General Daren Tang has been appointed for a second six-year term. Tang has championed global AI and IP policy harmonisation during his first term, including the WIPO Conversation on IP and AI and the 2024 WIPO Treaty on IP, Genetic Resources and Associated Traditional Knowledge. His reappointment signals continuity in WIPO's approach to balancing AI innovation with copyright protection — a key consideration for Prosus portfolio companies engaged in AI-assisted education, content, and ecommerce.

ip.title: Federal Circuit: Generic ML Application to New Data Environments Is Patent-Ineligible (Recentive v. Fox)
ip.body: On 18 April 2025, the US Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of Recentive Analytics' machine-learning patents (applied to TV scheduling and network mapping) under 35 U.S.C. § 101, holding that 'patents that do no more than claim the application of generic machine learning to new data environments are patent ineligible.' The court found the patents described conventional ML techniques (neural networks, decision trees) without disclosing specific technical improvements. The ruling constrains AI patent strategies that rely on applying standard models to novel domains — relevant to Prosus portfolio AI companies (CodeKarma, Advolve.AI, BrainLogic AI) seeking patent protection for domain-specific ML applications.

ai_landscape.title: Google Launches Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform at Cloud Next 2026 (April 22–24)
ai_landscape.body: At Google Cloud Next 2026 (22–24 April), Google launched the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, comprising Agent Studio (low-code builder), Agent Development Kit, and an Agent Marketplace featuring partners including Atlassian, Oracle, ServiceNow, and Workday. The platform includes governance tooling: Agent Identity, Agent Registry, and Agent Gateway for access control and observability across multi-agent deployments. Google simultaneously released Deep Research and Deep Research Max agents (powered by Gemini 3.1 Pro) with MCP support for autonomous web and proprietary data research. The launch intensifies the enterprise agentic AI race alongside OpenAI's Workspace Agents and Anthropic's Claude Managed Agents. Relevant to Prosus portfolio companies evaluating AI infrastructure for Ema, Clarity, and Orbii.

ai_landscape.title: OpenAI Releases GPT-5.5 and Workspace Agents Preview for Enterprise ChatGPT
ai_landscape.body: On 23 April 2026, OpenAI released GPT-5.5, positioning the company closer to an AI 'super app' combining multimodal generation, long-context reasoning, and tool use in a unified interface. Separately, on 22 April 2026, OpenAI launched a research preview of Workspace Agents in ChatGPT for Teams, enabling autonomous handling of complex organisational workflows via integration with Slack and shared Codex-powered agents — free until 6 May 2026. The dual launch reinforces OpenAI's push to embed agentic capabilities into enterprise productivity tools. On 15 April, OpenAI also updated its Agents SDK with native sandbox execution for long-horizon coding tasks. Relevant to Prosus portfolio companies exploring agentic AI deployment (Ema, CodeKarma).

ai_landscape.title: Meta Launches Muse Spark — First Model from Meta Superintelligence Labs with Multi-Agent Orchestration
ai_landscape.body: On 8 April 2026, Meta released Muse Spark, its first model from the newly established Meta Superintelligence Labs, featuring native multimodality, visual chain-of-thought, tool use, and a 'Contemplating' mode that launches parallel subagents. Muse Spark powers upgraded Meta AI across Instagram, Facebook, WhatsApp, and Ray-Ban smart glasses with Instant and Thinking modes. A private API preview is available for enterprise partners. The release escalates the foundation-model arms race and has regulatory implications: Meta AI's deep integration into WhatsApp is under active CADE scrutiny in Brazil (re: AI chatbot access), creating compliance risk for iFood and Movile, which rely on WhatsApp for customer communications.

ai_landscape.title: IAPP: Can AI Governance Catch Up to Innovation? View from DC (April 17, 2026)
ai_landscape.body: A 17 April 2026 IAPP analysis from Washington DC examines the growing gap between AI regulatory frameworks and the speed of agentic AI development, noting that the EU AI Act's GPAI obligations (effective August 2025) are being tested in real-time by models like Claude Mythos and GPT-5.5, while US federal AI governance remains fragmented across the FTC, NIST, and sector-specific regulators. The piece flags that no US federal AI liability framework exists for autonomous agents and that the EU is alone in attempting systemic risk assessments at pre-deployment stage. Relevant to Prosus as it scales AI portfolio companies (Ema, Clarity, Deccan AI) across multiple jurisdictions with diverging regulatory requirements.

ai_landscape.title: EU AI Act GPAI Code of Practice: Anthropic Engagement on Systemic Risk Model Obligations
ai_landscape.body: As of April 2026, the EU AI Office is actively engaging Anthropic on obligations under the GPAI Code of Practice (published July 2025) regarding its Claude model family — including Mythos, which the AI Office treats as a systemic risk GPAI candidate (>10^25 FLOPs threshold or equivalent capabilities). The Code requires signatories to provide unredacted access to the AI Office within 5 days of a request, conduct adversarial red-teaming, and publish safety/security frameworks. Anthropic has committed to the Code but is navigating tensions between EU transparency demands and US national security restrictions on Mythos access. The situation is a live test of the extraterritorial reach of the AI Act and the practical enforceability of the GPAI framework.

ai_landscape.title: Anthropic Publishes Research on Trustworthy Agents — Governance Principles for Agentic AI
ai_landscape.body: On 9 April 2026, Anthropic published research on trustworthy agents in practice, outlining five principles for safe agentic AI deployment: human control, values alignment, operational security, transparency, and privacy by design. The paper covers components of agent systems (model, harness, tools, environment) and references Claude Code and Claude Cowork as production implementations. The research is framed as both a product safety document and a policy contribution to ongoing EU AI Act GPAI classification debates. It is directly relevant to Prosus's AI portfolio (Ema, CodeKarma, Advolve.AI) and to governance frameworks being developed by the EU AI Office and OECD AI Observatory.
